title: Psalms 47
subtitle: God Has Ascended with a Shout

sections.header: Verses 1–5 — “Clap Your Hands, All You Nations”

heb.paragraph: "God has ascended (ʿālâ) amid shouts of joy, the LORD amid the sounding of trumpets" (v.5). An enthronement psalm. God ascends to his throne with shouting and trumpets. The church traditionally reads this for Christ’s ascension (Acts 1:9–11). The nations are invited: clap your hands, all peoples (v.1). God is king over all the earth (v.7).

notes.note: "Clap your hands, all you nations; shout to God with cries of joy. For the LORD Most High is awesome, the great King over all the earth." Universal summons. Not Israel alone but all nations.
"God has ascended amid shouts of joy." The ascension image. Whether describing the ark’s procession or a coronation, the church hears Christ ascending to the Father’s right hand.

refs.note: Paul's 'when he ascended on high' interprets Christ's ascension through the psalm's enthronement imagery.
Jesus ascending while disciples watch fulfills the psalm's vision of God going up with a shout.
Every knee bowing to the exalted name echoes the psalm's universal kingship theme.

hist.context: Psalm 47 is an enthronement psalm celebrating YHWH's kingship over all nations. Some scholars connect it to the Israelite New Year festival when the Ark was carried in procession, reenacting God's 'ascent' to his throne. The command to clap hands and shout suggests joyful corporate worship. 'God has ascended amid shouts of joy' (v.5) may reflect the Ark's entrance into the temple.

heb.gloss: he reigns / he has become king
heb.paragraph: "God reigns (mālak̅) over the nations; God is seated on his holy throne" (v.8). The enthronement declaration. "The nobles of the nations assemble as the people of the God of Abraham" (v.9). The Gentiles join Abraham’s family. The psalm envisions universal worship under one King — the missionary vision of the OT.

notes.note: "God is the King of all the earth; sing to him a psalm of praise. God reigns over the nations." The universal reign: not just Israel’s God but the world’s King.
"The nobles of the nations assemble as the people of the God of Abraham." Gentile inclusion in Abraham’s covenant. This is the Abrahamic promise (Gen 12:3) enacted in worship.

refs.note: The kingdom of the world becoming the kingdom of our Lord fulfills the psalm's universal reign theme.
The great multitude shouting 'Hallelujah! For our Lord God Almighty reigns' echoes the psalm's praise.
The King of kings title derives from the psalm's vision of God as king over all the earth.

hist.context: The reference to 'the nobles of the peoples gathered as the people of the God of Abraham' (v.9) envisions Gentile inclusion in Israel's worship. This universal scope anticipates prophetic visions of the nations streaming to Zion. The 'shields of the earth' (v.9) may refer to earthly rulers whose authority derives from the divine King.

ppl.name: God (Elohim / Yahweh / Most High)
ppl.role: Universal king who ascends his throne amid worship (vv.2,5-8)
ppl.text: God 'has ascended amid shouts of joy, the LORD amid the sounding of trumpets' (v.5) — possibly commemorating the ark's ascent to Zion (2 Sam 6) but pointing beyond it to God's universal kingship. He is 'the great King over all the earth' (v.2) and 'reigns over the nations' (v.8), sitting on his 'holy throne.' Christian liturgy has historically read the ascension language as prophetic of Christ's ascension (Acts 1:9-11), making this a traditional Ascension Day psalm.

ppl.name: Abraham
ppl.role: Patriarch whose promise finds fulfillment in gathered nations (v.9)
ppl.text: The psalm climaxes with 'the nobles of the nations assemble as the people of the God of Abraham' (v.9) — Gentile rulers joining Abraham's covenant community. This directly echoes God's promise to Abraham that 'all nations on earth will be blessed through you' (Gen 12:3). The psalm envisions the Abrahamic promise in its fullest scope: not Israel alone but the nations under Yahweh's reign.

ppl.name: The Sons of Korah
ppl.role: Korahite worship leaders directing universal praise (superscription)
ppl.text: The psalmists command 'all you nations, clap your hands; shout to God with cries of joy' (v.1) — an extraordinary summons extending Israelite worship to every people. This universalism within a Korahite psalm shows Israel's worship tradition reaching beyond ethnic boundaries. The psalm's brevity (9 verses) concentrates its theology: every line advances the argument for God's universal sovereignty.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVGod has ascended amid shouts of joy, the Lord amid the sounding of trumpets.ESVGod has gone up with a shout, the LORD with the sound of a trumpet.NIVFor the Lord Most High is awesome, the great King over all the earth.ESVFor the LORD, the Most High, is to be feared, a great king over all the earth.

rec.text: ""Clap your hands, all you nations; shout to God with cries of joy. For the LORD Most High is awesome, the great King over all the earth." Universal summons. Not Israel alone but all nations."

rows.label: Universal Reign — 47:6–9
rows.text: "God reigns over the nations." Nobles assemble as Abraham’s people.

hebtext.note: "God has ascended (ʿālâ) amid shouts of joy, the LORD amid the sounding of trumpets" (v.5). An enthronement psalm. God ascends to his throne with shouting and trumpets. The church traditionally reads this for Christ’s ascension (Acts 1:9–11). The nations are invited: clap your hands, all peoples (v.1). God is king over all the earth (v.7).

hebtext.note: "God reigns (mālak̅) over the nations; God is seated on his holy throne" (v.8). The enthronement declaration. "The nobles of the nations assemble as the people of the God of Abraham" (v.9). The Gentiles join Abraham’s family. themes.note: Sovereignty at 10: God reigns over all nations. Worship at 9: universal praise. Mission at 8: Gentiles join Abraham’s family. Prophecy: ascension points to Christ.
